comment.body:
“President von der Leyen held today a phone call with Premier Li Qiang to discuss the state of EU-China relations, as 2025 marks the 50th anniversary of diplomatic ties.

The two leaders held a constructive discussion during which they took stock of bilateral and global issues.

The President underscored the vital importance of stability and predictability for the global economy. In response to the widespread disruption caused by the US tariffs, President von der Leyen stressed the responsibility of Europe and China, as two of world's largest markets, to support a strong reformed trading system, free, fair and founded on a level playing field.

The President called for a negotiated resolution to the current situation, emphasising the need to avoid further escalation.

President von der Leyen emphasised China's critical role in addressing possible trade diversion caused by tariffs, especially in sectors already affected by global overcapacity. The leaders discussed setting up a mechanism for tracking possible trade diversion and ensuring any developments are duly addressed.

She also recalled the urgency for structural solutions to rebalance the bilateral trade relationship and ensure better access for European businesses, products and services to the Chinese market.

The conversation also covered EU-China cooperation on the international climate agenda and the clean industrial transition.

Finally, President von der Leyen reaffirmed the EU's steadfast support for a just and lasting peace in Ukraine, emphasising that any conditions for peace must be determined by Ukraine. She invited China to intensify its efforts to contribute meaningfully to the peace process.

Looking ahead, President von der Leyen noted that the upcoming EU-China Summit in July would be a fitting opportunity to commemorate the 50th anniversary of diplomatic relations.”

comment.body:
Good morning Alma and everyone.  Looks like a dead cat bounce in the works.  By the way, now that we are over a week removed from Trump's tariffs announcement, IMHO it's very important to re-read Alma's second to last weekly post.  

Alma, as I read that post again last night, I can't say enough about how insightful and intriguing it was to read with all of the news going out now.  One word: brilliant. 

For the rest of us, I am beginning to see how this market drop is going to bring about fear into the market related to a deflationary spiral, but as Alma writes about, that's what must happen.  The next sets of CPI and PCE prints may bring about this fear and are going to be so important.  This also fits into her thoughts about how we are not out of the woods, but just entering into it.  Therefore, building lifeboats is likely crucial over the course of the next days (weeks?). 

Lastly, the end game is this (and I will keep copying and pasting for all us to remember): the goal is a ceasefire and to defeat stagflation.  That will be the signal that Trump and the world powers can level off their tariff trade wars and shift into building up the market again.

comment.body:
Good morning Alma and everyone.  Looks like a dead cat bounce in the works.  By the way, now that we are over a week removed from Trump's tariffs announcement, IMHO it's very important to re-read Alma's second to last weekly post.  

Alma, as I read that post again last night, I can't say enough about how insightful and intriguing it was to read with all of the news going out now.  One word: brilliant. 

For the rest of us, I am beginning to see how this market drop is going to bring about fear into the market related to a deflationary spiral, but as Alma writes about, that's what must happen.  The next sets of CPI and PCE prints may bring about this fear and are going to be so important.  This also fits into her thoughts about how we are not out of the woods, but just entering into it.  Therefore, building lifeboats is likely crucial over the course of the next days (weeks?). 

Lastly, the end game is this (and I will keep copying and pasting for all us to remember): the goal is a ceasefire and to defeat stagflation.  That will be the signal that Trump and the world powers can level off their tariff trade wars and shift into building up the market again.

comment.body:
"Fed is behind ECB in dovish pivot but moving that way. We can expect the Fed start cutting by Q3 2025. It will also use tools to ensure liquidity – e.g. extending the BTFP (which lends to banks against bonds at par, essentially QE-like support). The Fed’s big concern is un-anchored inflation expectations, but so far long-run inflation expectations have remained around 2-2.5% (helped by the credibility they built)." "A dovish ECB would be interpreted as weakness that Trump could exploit.
An early ECB cut would support the idea that European nations are willing to compromise for short-term relief. That, in turn, creates a window for Trump to put Europe on the negotiation table—using the promise of stabilization as leverage for tax cuts or debt ceiling adjustments in the U.S.If Europe shows signs of sticking to high rates despite the trade war rather than easing, then that rigidity can actually worsen market sentiment."

comment.body:
Alma, 
After watching several times Ray Dalio’s videos on how the economy works, I assume that  we are currently in a debt deleveraging period.

Considering the fact that we are coming from a period of exorbitant liquidity injection and credit creation in the market (at unprecedented levels) and considering that rates are high and central banks still have room to operate, according to your posts, I began reasoning about a likely scenario:

In the video, Dalio discusses “non-beautiful” debt deleveraging, meaning that if the four tools used for debt deleveraging—cutting spending, reducing debt, redistributing wealth, and printing money—fail to offset the burden of rising debt on income values, then Trump’s target could be to force the ECB to cut interest rates. 
Given that the tariffs imposed by Trump on Europe might trigger a short-term peak in inflation—and since the ECB is mandated to control inflation—Trump could push the EU to lower rates, thereby giving him more negotiating leverage over Europe to force them to accept his conditions. 
Otherwise, Europe might adopt a tougher stance by compensating for the effects of the tariffs (which is what currently worries the markets the most!?) not through cutting rates, but by seeking trade deals with other partners, such as China.

I dropped all the reasonings i Made thanks to your posts. But at the end I come up thinking that we’re playing the match on what ECB will decide, whenever it will cut rate could be good scenario and viceversa. If it keep the rates steady it seem like the trade war can continue and escalate, because as we know what matters here is political ideology conflict.

comment.body:
Thinking contextually/critically and in a non binary view is something that isn’t necessarily hard for me. The real question is, what is the objective here? To build a trading plan around analysis that won’t be around after a year? Or to learn some skills where you can trade independently from the analysis and be self sustainable after a year by building that analysis yourself?

Would like some of the latter. But it is a good start to first be able to at least implement the analysis into an actionable trading plan. And that is still a tough task.

Now, as I’ve mentioned, Alma has natural talent… and talent, as I’ve said, is valued because it isn’t easily replicated. 

And Alma has been mentored, probably one on one, by some very talented people, in a setting where the screens were in front of her and she could actually learn on the fly, hands on.

So that’s something to keep in mind here is all I’m saying. It seems inevitable that if one becomes too complacent on Alma’s analysis, without understanding how to create it for yourself, then Alma goes from being a teacher, to an over nurturing “mommy”.
